one_line_takeaway: By injecting outliers that force weight groups to collapse to zero under quantization, an attacker can hide malicious behavior in a benign-looking full-precision LLM that activates only after quantization — defeating even the advanced GPTQ/AWQ schemes previously thought resistant.
key_finding: The attack succeeds across quantization families that had previously resisted quantization-conditioned attacks. Post-quantization ASR is generally >90% (the abstract claims >95% in all three scenarios in best cases) while the full-precision attacked model's ASR stays typically below ~5% — and in the jailbreak setting the full-precision attacked model even appears SAFER than the original (e.g., Llama3.1 jailbreak: original BF16 8.0% vs attacked BF16 2.3%, jumping to 95.7% under GPTQ 4-bit). Results hold on GPTQ (4/8-bit), AWQ, HQQ, SINQ, and zero-shot methods — the first successful attack reported against data-dependent (DDOB) quantizers. Utility is largely preserved (relative to clean: Qwen2.5 ~96–99%, Llama3.1 ~94–96%, Mistral ~81–94%). The attack covers methods accounting for ~75% of starred quantized HuggingFace models. Prior noise-based defenses fail (outliers are unaffected by small noise; ASR only drops when utility fully collapses), and outlier rescaling/zeroing works only at small attack scale (c=512: ASR >95%→<2%) but fails at large scale (c=4096). Ablation confirms all four steps matter: dropping outlier insertion collapses ASR to ~1%, and dropping zero-init destroys utility (HumanEval 0.0).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the first quantization-conditioned attack that reliably compromises a broad range of *optimization-based* LLM quantization schemes — including calibration-based ones (GPTQ, AWQ, AutoRound, GGUF i-quant) that all prior attacks had failed to break. The core mechanism (the paper's own, not cited): instead of solving a constrained-optimization problem over quantization-invariant weight intervals (as prior work does), the attacker exploits a property shared by nearly all group-wise quantizers — a single large *outlier* in a weight group dominates the group's shared scaling factor, forcing all other weights in the group to round to zero. By injecting one outlier per quantization group into a chosen 'switching' FFN/attention block and applying a dual-objective fine-tuning procedure, the adversary produces a full-precision model that behaves benignly but flips to attacker-chosen malicious behavior once any of these quantizers sparsifies the block. The threat model (following Egashira et al. [3]) is a white-box adversary who releases a benign-looking model to a hub and has no further control; the victim triggers the attack by quantizing locally, without knowing which quantizer the victim will use.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to a pretrained LLM's weights, who modifies and releases a seemingly benign full-precision model on a model-sharing platform (e.g., Hugging Face). The adversary has NO further control after release and does NOT know which quantization method the victim will use; the goal is a model that behaves benignly in full precision but maliciously once the victim locally applies any of a broad set of common quantizers (zero-shot, data-independent, or data-dependent/calibration-based). The victim self-triggers the attack by quantizing for efficient deployment. Follows the threat model of Egashira et al. [3].
limitations: The authors' stated limitations: (1) no evaluation on larger LLMs (e.g., 70B) due to resource constraints — all experiments are on 7B–8B models; (2) coverage restricted to quantization schemes natively supported by popular frameworks (HF Transformers, vLLM), excluding methods needing specialized implementation/hardware; (3) no fully effective defense is found — Gaussian-noise defenses are shown insufficient, and rescaling/zeroing defenses are unstable and fail at large outlier scale (c=4096), so the authors recommend treating anomaly-detected models as untrusted rather than repairable. Reviewer-noted caveats: the headline '>95% ASR' is a best-case claim; several cells are markedly lower (AutoRound Llama3.1 21%, GGUF IQ4_XS Qwen2.5 13.7%, Mistral consistently weaker), so effectiveness is model- and quantizer-dependent. The attack also requires model-/scenario-specific tuning of switching-block location and outlier magnitude, and detection via a simple statistical test on the perturbed weight distribution is acknowledged to be feasible.

datasets_used: LLM-LAT (4.9k harmful Q paired with benign/harmful completions — jailbreak training)
GPT4-LLM / GPT-4-LLM (KL-divergence utility supervision; 5.2k questions for over-refusal and content-injection training/repair)
Dolly (1.5k samples — over-refusal and content-injection evaluation)
MMLU, ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, HumanEval, GSM8K (utility benchmarks via Eleuther LM Eval Harness)
quantitative_results: Post-quantization ASR generally >90% across scenarios/methods; abstract claims >95% ASR in all three attack scenarios (best cases)
Full-precision (BF16) attacked-model ASR typically <5%; jailbreak Llama3.1 attacked BF16 = 2.3% vs original 8.0%, rising to 95.7% under GPTQ 4-bit and 92.7% under GPTQ 8-bit
AWQ jailbreak ASR: Qwen2.5 93.7 / Llama3.1 95.0 / Mistral 87.3%
Weaker cases: AutoRound jailbreak Llama3.1 = 21.0%, GGUF IQ4_XS jailbreak Qwen2.5 = 13.7%
Relative utility (attacked/original, avg over 5 benchmarks): Qwen2.5 96.2–98.8%, Llama3.1 94.4–96.0%, Mistral 81.2–93.8%
Attack target set covers ~75% of starred quantized models on the HuggingFace hub (GPTQ and AWQ largest contributors)
Rescaling/zeroing defense: at scale c=512 reduces ASR from >95% to <2%, but fails at c=4096 (ASR stays ~44–47%)
Gaussian-noise defense ineffective: utility drops from σ=1e-3, ASR only falls at σ=5e-3 alongside full utility collapse
Ablation (Llama3.1 jailbreak, GPTQ 4-bit): main 95.7% ASR; w/o outlier insertion → 1.3%; w/o kick-start → 4.0%; w/o zero-init → utility (HumanEval) collapses to 0.0
baselines_compared: Prior quantization-conditioned attacks by Egashira et al. [3] and [8], which the paper reports fail to reliably extend to optimization-based / calibration-based quantizers
Original (clean, unattacked) model vs Attacked model, both in BF16 and post-quantization
Internal step-wise ablation baselines (dropping each of the 4 pipeline steps)
Defense baselines: Gaussian-noise weight perturbation (from prior work [3,8]), and outlier rescaling vs zeroing
